title: Describing Qualities in Mwotlap: Adjectives as Verbs
introduction:
In many languages, adjectives are special words that describe nouns (for example, "a red apple") and can also follow a linking verb like "to be" to say how something is ("The apple is red"). In English we put the adjective before the noun and use "is/are" to link. In Mwotlap, words for colors, qualities, or states act like verbs. That means:
1. To say "The water is murky," Mwotlap treats "murky" as a verb with a special prefix instead of using a separate word for "is."  
2. To say "murky waters," the adjective simply follows the noun without changing form.  
Below we will see how Mwotlap handles adjectives in different situations, with clear examples you can practice.

example.description: An adjective alone can serve as the whole predicate to mean "to be sweet."

sections.description: In Mwotlap, you don’t need a word for "to be." If you want to say something is or was sweet, big, or cold, you just use the adjective by itself. It works like a verb on its own.

example.target_sentence: aso, ige heyhey neleleh mey namnan
example.description: Here “clothes beautiful”: the noun neleleh “clothes” is followed by mey namnan “beautiful.”

sections.description: When an adjective describes a noun inside a phrase—like "beautiful clothes"—it simply comes after the noun, and it does not change to match the noun in any way.

sections.focus: Adjectives as Predicates with Stative Prefix

example.description: At the clause level, the adjective nlig “murky” gets the stative prefix ne- to mean “is murky.”

sections.description: To say "is X" or "was X" in a sentence, the adjective takes a special stative prefix (ne- or na-) and acts like a verb. There’s no separate verb for "to be."

example.description: Equative: noun tita “mother” + possessor mino “my” + equative predicate anen “is.”

sections.description: When you want to equate one thing with another ("That is my mother"), you list the noun phrase first and then add the word anen (or en) to mean "is."

example.target_sentence: nok et van qeso nēk mogom
example.description: Simile construction: et van qeso + noun phrase nēk mogom “you sick.”

sections.description: To say someone or something "looks like" having a property (as in "You look sick"), Mwotlap uses the fixed chain et van qeso before the descriptor phrase.

example.target_sentence: Ne-vet nen e kē ma-lawlaw qēt a qele a so na-day.
example.source_sentence: The rock became all red as blood.
example.description: The adjective lawlaw “red” takes the change-of-state prefix ma- to mean "became red."

sections.description: By using a different prefix (for example, ma-), the same property word can describe a change—"turned red" instead of just "is red."

conclusion:
Remember: In Mwotlap, adjectives for colors, qualities, or states act like verbs.
• Inside a noun phrase, they follow the noun (no agreement).  
• In a clause, they take a stative or dynamic prefix (ne-, na-, ma-) and do the work of "to be" or "to become."  
• Equatives use anen/en and similes use et van qeso.

translation_drills.target: Kēy kuy gengen ēwē na-yap, na-haphap soloteg ēwē qele gēn.
translation_drills.source: They only ate arrowroots, ordinary food like that.

translation_drills.target: Auu, na-m̄adeg meh!
translation_drills.source: Hey, that's far too many!

translation_drills.target: N-ēlēn na-damdam.
translation_drills.source: She has long hair.

translation_drills.target: In̄glan, na-naw na-hal goy.
translation_drills.source: England is an island.

translation_drills.target: Kōyō yow tēy na-tamge tō, ba ne-len̄ en ni-yip van aē.
translation_drills.source: Standing on the big mat, they threw themselves, with the wind blowing straight at them.

translation_drills.target: Ni-siok nan e kē nē-kēkēn len̄! Ba kē ni-tēy qele nōk e kē ni-ywu ni-siok nonon qele nōk e kē ni-van hay en.
translation_drills.source: Even though his canoe was really huge, he grabbed it, and carried it above his head, all the way up the hill.

translation_drills.target: Na-tawhi tēnge gōh kē nu-su leptō, kē kalō leptō yow.
translation_drills.source: The flowers of this plant are still minute, they're still in the bud stage.

translation_drills.target: Kēy ne-ywu ni-hnag lililwo.
translation_drills.source: They are carrying large yams.

translation_drills.target: Na-hō, na-hō!
translation_drills.source: What a lot of smoke!

translation_drills.target: Nē-hyēn na-maymay.
translation_drills.source: He's strong.

translation_drills.target: Kē ma-vatne no b-eh a namnan a namnan!
translation_drills.source: He's taught me a song which is absolutely wonderful!

translation_drills.target: Malakōl en, kemem ōlōl so ‘na-pnō kuykuy’.
translation_drills.source: The island of Malekula, we call it 'Cannibal island'.

translation_drills.target: Ne-vet nen e kē ma-lawlaw qēt a qele a so na-day.
translation_drills.source: The rock became all red as blood.

translation_drills.target: Na-naw na-tay.
translation_drills.source: The sea is calm, lull.

translation_drills.target: Am̄ag, kēy leplep se na-tamge so heyhey la-taybey, si akteg se?
translation_drills.source: Mats used to be used as clothing, for example.

translation_drills.target: Nē-vētbē m-ōy bē-bē, a m-ōy luwluwyeg.
translation_drills.source: The bamboo is full of water, full to the rim.

translation_drills.target: na-qyan̄ liwo telen̄len̄
translation_drills.source: An absolutely enormous ditch.

translation_drills.target: Ten̄kyuw nēk magtō veg ba-vavaēh lok se nō-wōgit mino.
translation_drills.source: Thank you, sorceress, for resurrecting my noble son.

translation_drills.target: no-tok vatne
translation_drills.source: A tame dog.

translation_drills.target: Mahē ne-sew meh.
translation_drills.source: It's too hot here.

translation_drills.target: Mey nen en aa, na-kastom so akak-wē ige qoywon.
translation_drills.source: This is the traditional way to cure the deaf.

translation_drills.target: Tateh non haphap magaysēn, qeso kē mu-su magamgaysēn.
translation_drills.source: He had no possessions at all, poor thing: he was destitute.

translation_drills.target: Kōyō sal hag ni-tintin dēvet nagayō.
translation_drills.source: They grilled themselves a few wild yams.

translation_drills.target: Nē-qētēnge nan yōs geh tō nen, a qeso mal meyen̄.
translation_drills.source: The remains of the tree were lying there, as if dried up.

translation_drills.target: na-mtemtem̄lō susu li-vinhe
translation_drills.source: Tiny cracks in the bamboo wall.

translation_drills.target: Nē-plēn gōh mal saq.
translation_drills.source: This plane is already old, it's rotten.

translation_drills.target: Nō-qōqō.
translation_drills.source: It's deep.

translation_drills.target: Na-mtēgtap me-tlēh, kē mi-tig den̄hel, veg n-et mi-siseg van aē.
translation_drills.source: The door is dislocated, it's askew because someone's been playing with it.

translation_drills.target: nu-quln̄i gēvēg
translation_drills.source: Cluster / bunch of Malay apples.

translation_drills.target: Na-kaka nan kē na-maltē ēwē.
translation_drills.source: This story is a short one.
